Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Islamic Tradition

Mullā Ṣadrā

c. 1571 – 1640

Substantial motion, soul, and consciousness as intensified being.

Mullā Ṣadrā’s philosophy of mind cannot be extracted by isolating “mental states” from theology, metaphysics, ethics, and eschatology. His theory is built on the claim that mind is a mode of being: consciousness, knowledge, selfhood, imagination, intellect, angelic cognition, and divine knowledge are all treated as degrees or modalities of wujūd—existence—rather than as a sealed-off psychological domain. The most concise reconstruction is this: mind is existence intensified into presence; the human soul is a body-born process that becomes immaterial; cognition is an existential act in which the knower is transformed by the known; divine and angelic intellects are not theological ornaments but the upper limits and explanatory poles of the whole theory. This reconstruction fits the major English-language scholarship, Persian Sadrian reception, Arabic discussions of divine knowledge and the soul, French Corbin/Jambet-inspired readings of presence and imagination, and Turkish work on the mind–body problem.

A terminological caution is essential. “Mind” is not a single Sadrian term. The technical center is nafs, the soul or self; ʿaql is intellect; dhihn is mind in the sense of mental existence; khayāl is imagination; qalb sometimes names the heart as a locus of spiritual awareness; and ʿilm means knowledge but, in Ṣadrā’s deepest usage, also a mode of existential presence. A Sadrian philosophy of mind therefore has to treat “mind” as a layered structure: vegetative animation, animal sensation, imaginal form-production, rational intellection, self-presence, and finally participation in higher intellective realities.

1. The metaphysical ground: existence, gradation, and motion

Ṣadrā’s psychology rests on three metaphysical claims. First, existence is ontologically prior to essence: essences are not the primary realities, but ways in which existence is delimited, conceived, or made intelligible. Second, existence is gradational: beings differ not merely by kind but by intensity, priority, perfection, and weakness. Third, material substances are not static substrates with changing accidents; they undergo substantial motion, meaning that the very substance of a thing is in ontological becoming. These doctrines make it possible for Ṣadrā to say that the soul begins with the body but can become a higher, immaterial mode of existence without ceasing to be one continuous self.

This is the point at which Ṣadrā’s philosophy becomes unusually important for philosophy of mind. If existence itself admits degrees, then “mental” and “bodily” need not be two absolutely alien substances. The bodily, the sensory, the imaginal, and the intellective can be understood as levels of one developmental arc. The human person is not a Cartesian composite of an already complete immaterial ego plus an external machine-body; nor is the person reducible to physiology. The soul is an emergent-but-not-physicalist reality: it emerges through the body’s own substantial motion, then surpasses the bodily level by becoming increasingly immaterial. Turkish scholarship on Ṣadrā’s nefs–beden relation captures this well: his formula of corporeal origination and spiritual subsistence is presented as a third alternative to strict hylomorphism and strict dualism.

The metaphysical correlate of consciousness is presence. Matter is associated with dispersion, potentiality, and lack of self-presence; immateriality is associated with actuality, unity, and self-presence. This is why Ṣadrā can make knowledge a metaphysical rather than merely epistemological phenomenon. A French presentation of Jambet’s reading states that for Ṣadrā knowledge is an existential experience of the soul, an immediate disclosure of the presence of a being, whether one’s own being or another’s. The same source distinguishes external corporeal existence, mental existence, and cognitive existence, emphasizing that cognitive existence is a real mode of being, not merely a ghostly copy inside the head.

2. The soul: “corporeal in origination, spiritual in survival”

Ṣadrā’s famous formula is jismāniyyat al-ḥudūth wa-rūḥāniyyat al-baqāʾ: the soul is corporeal in its origination and spiritual in its subsistence or survival. This does not mean that an immaterial soul is inserted into a body from outside, nor that the soul is merely a bodily function. It means that the individual soul comes to be with and through the body, then undergoes an intensifying process by which it becomes capable of immaterial cognition and post-mortem subsistence. The Stanford account notes that Ṣadrā distinguishes the pre-existence of the “Adamic soul” as a kind or category from the non-pre-existence of individual human souls, partly because he rejects metempsychosis.

The developmental sequence is crucial. In Ṣadrā’s account, the soul first appears as a corporeal faculty, then a natural form, then a sensing soul, then reflective and recollective powers, then rational soul, and finally practical and theoretical intellect moving toward actualization. This is not a mere biological maturation story; it is an ontological ascent. The same individual existence becomes more intense, more unified, and more capable of presence.

Arabic and Persian sources preserve the formula in a way that makes its cognitive significance explicit. One Arabic source states that the human soul is “corporeal in origination and governance, spiritual in survival and intellection,” adding that its governance of bodies is corporeal while its intellection of itself and its maker is spiritual. The same passage also records the Sadrian principle al-nafs fī waḥdatihā kull al-quwā: the soul, in its unity, is all the faculties. This principle prevents Ṣadrā’s psychology from becoming a theory of separate mental modules; the faculties are powers, levels, or branches of one self.

This theory also gives Ṣadrā a distinctive answer to the mind–body relation. The relation is not like a carpenter using a saw, nor like a ghost inhabiting a machine; it is closer to the relation between matter and form, but with a dynamic twist. Persian discussions of the Sadrian soul–body problem emphasize that earlier philosophers often explained the soul’s relation to the body as governance, attachment, or instrumental control, whereas Ṣadrā’s doctrine of bodily origination makes the connection intrinsic to the soul’s very coming-to-be. Turkish discussions similarly describe the relation as hylomorphic and unitive at the level of genesis, while noting that once the rational soul reaches its own level it no longer depends on worldly matter in the same way.

3. What kind of “mind–body theory” is this?

A Sadrian philosophy of mind is neither reductive physicalism nor standard substance dualism. It affirms an immaterial soul, so it is not physicalist. But it denies that the human soul begins as a separately complete immaterial substance merely attached to the body, so it is not Cartesian or Platonic dualism either. The best modern label is approximate: dynamic, gradational, non-reductive, hylomorphic spiritual emergentism. “Emergentism” here must be used carefully, because Ṣadrā’s emergence is not an emergence from matter conceived as self-sufficient physical stuff; it is emergence within a metaphysics where matter itself is a weak, potential, moving mode of existence.

The soul’s relation to the body is therefore diachronic. At the beginning, the body is not an external container; the soul’s individualization and early actualization occur through bodily organization. As the soul intensifies, it becomes capable of immaterial acts—especially imagination and intellection—that are no longer reducible to bodily organs. This means that neuroscience, in a Sadrian framework, can explain the bodily conditions of cognition but cannot exhaust the reality of cognition, because the higher acts of the soul are not simply neural events. This conclusion follows from Ṣadrā’s claim that the soul’s later survival and intellection are spiritual, not from a denial that the body is necessary for the soul’s origination.

The formula also resolves, or at least reframes, psychophysical causation. Ṣadrā does not need a mysterious causal bridge between two complete substances. The vegetative, sensory, imaginal, and rational powers are levels of one moving existence. When bodily processes condition perception, they do so because the lower levels of the soul are bodily-involved; when intellective acts transcend matter, they do so because the soul has intensified beyond its initial bodily level. Persian Sadrian sources explicitly connect the solution of soul–body difficulties with bodily origination, the intrinsic relation of soul and body, the unity of the soul’s faculties, and substantial motion.

4. Consciousness as graded self-presence

Ṣadrā’s most radical move is to make consciousness a matter of existential intensity. The Stanford article even describes a Sadrian “pan-psychism” in which all existents possess some degree of sentience and aspire toward higher intensity, because existence, intellect, and soul are intimately connected. This should not be flattened into contemporary micro-qualia panpsychism. Ṣadrā is not saying that electrons have little private sensations in the modern analytic sense. He is saying that existence itself has inwardness or presence in proportion to its immateriality and intensity; matter has this only weakly and dispersedly, while immaterial intellects have it strongly.

Self-knowledge is the privileged case. Ṣadrā’s theory of ʿilm ḥuḍūrī, knowledge by presence, says that in self-awareness the known is present to the knower without an intervening representation. Contemporary comparative work on Ṣadrā and Sydney Shoemaker has argued that Ṣadrā’s doctrine helps explain why “self-blindness” to some mental states is impossible: in basic self-awareness, the state is not inferred, represented, or observed from outside; it is present in the very being of the subject.

Knowledge by presence has several features. It is immediate, because the known itself is present rather than a substitute image. It is non-representational, because no mental form stands between knower and known. It is infallible in a restricted sense, because truth and falsity arise where a representation may or may not correspond to what it represents; in pure presence, there is no such gap. It is also ontological, not merely psychological: the relation between knower and known is a relation of being. This does not mean that every judgment one makes about oneself is incorrigible. It means that the basic givenness of one’s present state is not mediated by representation.

This theory is especially relevant to philosophy of mind because it rejects the “inner sense” model. The self does not know itself by turning an internal eye upon an inner object. Rather, the self is manifest to itself. A French review of Jambet’s work makes this point through Suhrawardī’s influence on Ṣadrā: one does not need anything other than one’s essence manifesting itself to itself in order to perceive oneself.

5. Acquired knowledge, mental existence, and intentionality

Ṣadrā distinguishes knowledge by presence from ʿilm ḥuṣūlī, acquired or representational knowledge. Acquired knowledge involves a form through which something is known. But Ṣadrā’s account is not a simple “mental picture” theory. He develops the doctrine of wujūd dhihnī, mental existence, and more deeply wujūd ʿilmī, cognitive existence: the known has a mode of existence in the soul. This allows the mind to know things even when they do not exist externally, while preserving the idea that cognition is not sheer nothingness or a merely verbal relation.

This is where Ṣadrā’s intentionality theory becomes powerful. To know an object is not merely to stand in an external relation to it; it is for the object, in its cognitive mode of existence, to be present in the soul. The soul is not a passive screen on which forms are projected. It actively generates or receives cognitive forms in a way that actualizes its own being. In one classical presentation, the human soul’s knowledge of things is compared analogically to divine creative knowledge: God’s knowledge brings about objective existence, while human knowledge brings about mental existence. The analogy is not equality; it is a structural comparison between divine creative knowing and human form-producing cognition.

Ṣadrā’s famous doctrine of the unity of intellect and intelligible, ittiḥād al-ʿāqil wa-l-maʿqūl, should be read in this context. It does not mean that when I know a tree, my external body becomes identical with the external tree, nor that two quiddities collapse into one. It means that the soul, as knower, and the intelligible form, as known-in-act, are one in cognitive existence. Persian scholarship states the point clearly: Ṣadrā’s claim is that every intelligible existence is also intellecting, and that every form arising in the rational soul—whether intelligible or sensible—is one with its perceiver.

An Arabic edition of Ṣadrā’s philosophical treatise on the unity of knower and known gives the same core argument. A soul that is not yet intellect-in-act cannot possess actual intelligibles as something wholly alien to it; when intelligible forms are actual, they are intelligible in themselves, and the soul that knows them must be united with them in the relevant cognitive-existential sense. This is why knowledge is transformative: the soul becomes what it knows, not by losing itself, but by intensifying itself through the known.

6. Learning as ontological transformation

Because knowledge is existential, learning is not the addition of information to an unchanged container. It is the perfection of the soul. Ṣadrā’s view is not merely that philosophy gives correct propositions, but that philosophy heals, perfects, and intensifies the soul. The Stanford article notes that philosophy, for Ṣadrā, is a process of perfecting the soul through knowledge, and knowledge has a transformative effect on an ignorant soul or mind.

This has a major implication for philosophy of mind: cognitive development is ontological development. The soul’s movement from potential intellect to actual intellect is not merely skill acquisition; it is a change in the soul’s degree of being. In the highest form of intellection, the soul approaches or unites with the Active Intellect, described in the Sadrian tradition as a transcendent principle of perfect knowledge. This is not a decorative Neoplatonic remnant; it is part of the architecture of cognition. The individual intellect becomes actual by participating in a higher noetic order.

The doctrine also explains why Ṣadrā’s psychology is inseparable from ethics. What the soul attends to, desires, imagines, and understands shapes what the soul becomes. A life dominated by appetite, fantasy, or error does not merely contain bad mental contents; it forms a lower kind of self. A life ordered toward intellective truth and spiritual perception forms a higher self. This is why Ṣadrā’s philosophy of mind has an intrinsic moral and eschatological dimension.

7. Perception: sense, imagination, and intellect

Ṣadrā’s psychology works with a hierarchy of cognitive powers: sense, imagination, and intellect. These are not sealed compartments. They correspond to levels of reality: the sensible world, the imaginal or intermediary world, and the intelligible world. Persian reception influenced by Corbin emphasizes that for Ṣadrā the three faculties of knowledge—sense, imagination, and intellect—correspond to three worlds, and that imagination is a genuine cognitive faculty proper to its own world.

Sense perception is the lowest cognitive level, but even here Ṣadrā is not a crude empiricist. The soul is not simply stamped by external objects. Sensory cognition involves the soul’s own act under bodily conditions. The external object and sense organs are not irrelevant, but the perceived form as cognized belongs to the soul’s cognitive existence. This is consistent with Ṣadrā’s broader claim that knowledge is not a passive accident inhering in the soul but an existential actualization of it.

Imagination is one of Ṣadrā’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. In many earlier philosophical accounts, imagination is tied to bodily organs and therefore perishes with the body. Ṣadrā argues instead that the imaginative faculty is immaterial or spiritually surviving. This allows him to explain dreams, visionary experience, prophecy, resurrection, and post-mortem embodiment without reducing them to either physical sensation or abstract intellection.

The imaginal world, ʿālam al-mithāl, is not “imaginary” in the sense of unreal. It is an intermediate mode of being in which forms have body-like manifestation without material density. The Stanford account notes that the imaginal mediates between the intelligible world of concepts and the sensible world of things, and it allows abstract realities such as fear and desire to take corporeal or quasi-corporeal features in resurrection. French Corbinian reception similarly treats Ṣadrā’s metaphysics of resurrection as grounded in spiritual body and the imaginal world.

This gives Ṣadrā a distinctive answer to the status of mental imagery. Mental images are not merely subjective decorations. At lower levels, they may be tied to bodily sensation and memory; at higher levels, imagination discloses an objective imaginal order. Prophetic vision, for example, is not treated as hallucination but as the perfected operation of an imaginal faculty capable of receiving or manifesting higher forms. This claim is metaphysically loaded, but within Ṣadrā’s system it is a central theory of cognition, not an optional theological add-on.

8. Prophecy and extraordinary cognition as philosophy of perception

Ṣadrā’s theory of prophecy is best understood as an extension of his philosophy of perception. In one account of his philosophy of perception, the perfection of the human intellect is to witness the angels drawn near to God; the perfection of the form-bearing imaginative faculty is to witness archetypal forms and encounter unseen things; and the perfection of sense involves an intensified influence over bodily matter. The prophet unites the perfection of intellect, imagination, and sense.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it means Ṣadrā does not draw the modern boundary between “ordinary cognition” and “religious experience” in the usual way. The prophetic mind is not a psychological anomaly but the fully intensified mind. Revelation is analyzed through faculties: intellect receives intelligibles, imagination gives form to what is received, and sense may become unusually powerful. Whether one accepts the metaphysics or not, the account is philosophically systematic.

The same structure helps explain dreams and visions. Ordinary people may encounter the imaginal in sleep, while prophets and saints can encounter it in wakefulness. The difference is not between real and unreal, but between weak and perfected access to a level of being. This is why the imaginal faculty is indispensable to Ṣadrā’s full account of mind.

9. Personal identity, memory, and post-mortem embodiment

Ṣadrā’s theory of personal identity follows from his doctrine that existence, not matter, individuates. The body’s matter changes constantly, but the soul’s individual existence persists through developmental transformation. The Stanford account says that the identity of the body is determined by its soul rather than by materiality, and that human identity remains continuous from the womb through childhood, old age, death, and the afterlife.

The afterlife body is especially important for philosophy of mind. Ṣadrā argues that the soul retains creative power after bodily death and produces a body from its memory of prior embodiment. The resurrected body is not the dense physical body of this world; it is “born of the perfected soul” and corresponds to another level of existence. Persian discussions summarize the point by saying that after separation from the elemental body, the soul preserves the image of its worldly body, and through the surviving imaginative faculty a corresponding body proceeds from the soul.

This makes memory and imagination metaphysically constitutive. Memory is not merely storage; it contributes to the soul’s post-mortem self-manifestation. Imagination is not merely fantasy; it is the power through which the soul can form an embodied world appropriate to its own state. In a Sadrian philosophy of mind, embodiment is therefore plural: there is material embodiment, imaginal embodiment, and spiritual-intellective embodiment.

10. Divine knowledge: the “mind of God” handled carefully

It is better not to speak of “the mind of God” in a casual anthropomorphic way. Ṣadrā’s proper topic is divine knowledge, divine intellect, or God as simple self-knowing existence. God is not a psychological subject among other subjects, not a cosmic brain, and not an entity that forms representations. God is pure actuality and necessary existence; divine knowledge is identical with the divine essence. The Stanford article reports Ṣadrā’s claim that God’s knowledge of all things is simple, and that knowledge is an expression for existence insofar as existence is not mixed with matter.

The problem of God’s knowledge of particulars is central. Ṣadrā inherits the Avicenna–Ghazālī dispute over whether God knows particulars as particulars, and he answers by tying knowledge to presence, causality, and the simplicity of existence. Persian discussions of divine knowledge in transcendent philosophy state that God’s knowledge, however conceived, is presential; that God’s knowledge of Himself is identical with His essence; that God knows what is other than Himself before creation; and that this knowledge is “summary” or simple while also disclosing details.

Arabic discussions of divine knowledge in Ṣadrā describe his doctrine as “detailed knowledge united with the essence”: God’s knowledge of Himself is identical with His detailed knowledge of things, while remaining one simple reality. Persian accounts also note that Ṣadrā’s statements on God’s knowledge of material things can appear to vary, especially over whether material existents themselves are present to God or whether their immaterial forms are the direct objects of divine knowledge. This is an important interpretive tension, not a trivial footnote.

For philosophy of mind, divine knowledge is the limiting case of non-representational cognition. Human cognition produces mental existence; divine cognition produces or grounds objective existence. One classical account says that God sees the forms of all things in His own essence and brings them into being, while the human soul contemplates forms in the mirror of its own being and produces mental rather than external existence. This analogy must be handled reverently and asymmetrically: human knowing resembles divine knowing structurally, but God’s knowledge is infinite, simple, creative, and identical with His being.

The “mind of God,” then, is not a detachable theological distraction. It is the upper limit of Ṣadrā’s theory of mind: perfect self-presence, perfect non-representational knowledge, and creative causality. Without this divine model, the Sadrian account of knowledge by presence, the unity of knower and known, and the soul’s creative power become much harder to understand.

11. Angelic intellects and the minds of angels

Angelic or separate intellects are also philosophically central. They are not merely devotional figures added to an otherwise secular theory. In Ṣadrā’s hierarchy, immaterial beings such as angels are pure actualities relative to material beings: they do not undergo substantial motion in the way material beings do, because they are not composites of actuality and potentiality in the same way. Yet they are not absolutely perfect, because only the Necessary Being is pure absolute actuality.

Their “minds” are not private Cartesian interiors. An angelic intellect is a mode of immaterial existence whose knowledge is bound to its being. In the case of pure intellects, intellect and intelligible are the same in a way more complete than in ordinary human cognition, though still not identical with God’s absolute self-knowledge. One classical account distinguishes pure intellects from God by saying that their knowledge is identical with their being but not with their quiddities, whereas in God knowledge is identical with being and essence because God’s essence is His existence.

The Active Intellect is especially important. In Avicennan and Sadrian contexts, it is the transcendent source or locus of intelligibles and often overlaps with angelological language. Ṣadrā’s human intellect does not merely receive inert data; it becomes actual by union with higher intelligible reality. The Stanford account states that true knowledge resides in uniting with the Active Intellect, while preserving distinctions through modulation and degrees.

Prophecy shows the practical role of angelic intellects. Ṣadrā describes the perfection of the human intellect as witnessing the angels drawn near to God, while prophetic imagination can witness unseen archetypal forms and receive revelatory auditions through angelic intermediaries. Thus angelic minds are part of the epistemology of revelation and the metaphysics of intellectual perfection.

This makes the human–angel comparison philosophically rich. Angels are more purely intellective; humans are developmental beings who begin bodily and ascend. The human soul’s mixed corporeal-spiritual path gives it a distinctive drama: it can fall into lower animalized or distorted forms, but it can also intensify toward angelic and intellective modes of being. In Ṣadrā’s system, the angelic is not an external mythic category; it is a real noetic pole of the human mind’s possible perfection.

12. The complete Sadrian philosophy of mind in systematic form

A comprehensive Sadrian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed around twelve theses.

First, mind is not a substance separate from being; it is a mode and intensity of being. The more immaterial and unified a being is, the more it is present to itself and capable of knowledge.

Second, the human mind is the soul in its cognitive development. It begins in bodily life, passes through sense and imagination, and may culminate in intellective self-presence.

Third, the mind–body relation is developmental and unitive rather than an external interaction between two complete substances. The soul originates corporeally, but its higher acts are spiritual.

Fourth, consciousness is self-presence. Basic self-awareness is not representational, observational, or inferential; it is the soul’s presence to itself.

Fifth, intentionality is cognitive existence. The known object exists in the soul in a cognitive mode, and this explains how thought can be about things beyond immediate bodily contact, including absent or non-existent objects.

Sixth, knowledge is transformative. The knower is not unchanged by knowing; in genuine intellection the soul is actualized by the intelligible. This is the philosophical force of the unity of knower and known.

Seventh, representation is real but secondary. Acquired knowledge uses forms and can be true or false; knowledge by presence is more fundamental and anchors self-knowledge and the highest metaphysical knowledge.

Eighth, imagination is a real cognitive power, not merely fantasy. It has an ontological domain, survives bodily death, and mediates dreams, visions, prophecy, resurrection, and imaginal embodiment.

Ninth, intellect is not merely discursive reasoning. Discursive thought is one level; the perfected intellect participates in simple, non-discursive knowledge and union with higher intelligible reality.

Tenth, personal identity is existential rather than material. The self persists through bodily change because its individual existence is carried by the soul, not by stable matter.

Eleventh, divine knowledge is the supreme case of mind as presence. God does not represent things from outside; His knowledge is simple, self-identical, creative, and the ground of all objectivity.

Twelfth, angelic intellects are genuine noetic realities. They are not distractions from philosophy of mind but part of the hierarchy that explains intellectual perfection, prophecy, and the soul’s ascent.

13. Comparison with modern options

Compared with reductive physicalism, Ṣadrā rejects the claim that mental life can be fully explained by matter in its lowest mode. Matter is too dispersed and potential to account for self-presence, intellective universality, and immaterial cognition. However, Ṣadrā does not deny the body’s importance; the individual human soul begins through the body, and bodily organization is necessary for the early stages of cognition.

Compared with Cartesian dualism, Ṣadrā rejects a static gap between an immaterial mind and an extended body. The human soul is not an already complete thinking substance that merely uses the body. It becomes itself through bodily origination and substantial motion. Yet Ṣadrā remains an immaterialist about the mature rational and imaginal powers, so he cannot be assimilated to modern naturalism.

Compared with Aristotelian hylomorphism, Ṣadrā accepts the importance of form and matter but radicalizes the account through substantial motion and spiritual survival. The soul is not simply the form of a living body in a way that ends with bodily death; it is a moving existence that begins as bodily form and can become independent of matter.

Compared with phenomenology, Ṣadrā offers a strong theory of pre-reflective self-awareness. His knowledge by presence resembles the idea that the subject is not first hidden from itself and then discovered by introspection. But Ṣadrā’s account is more metaphysical: self-presence is grounded in immaterial being, not merely in the structure of intentional consciousness.

Compared with contemporary panpsychism, Ṣadrā’s view is more hierarchical and teleological. All beings have some inwardness or sentience insofar as they exist, but the meaningful degrees of consciousness depend on intensity, unity, and immateriality. This is not a flat distribution of experience across matter; it is an ascending order from weak presence to divine self-presence.

14. Interpretive tensions and open problems

The first major tension concerns the meaning of “corporeal origination.” Does Ṣadrā mean that the soul is literally material at first, or that it is a bodily-dependent form with the potential for immateriality? Persian sources note that commentators have debated the exact meaning of “corporeality” in the formula, and Turkish sources observe that the formula can look dualist if one ignores Ṣadrā’s insistence that the soul remains one reality through different degrees.

The second tension concerns the unity of knower and known. Ṣadrā must avoid the absurd claim that two already actual, independent things become one in every respect. Persian scholarship stresses that Ṣadrā rejects that kind of union; the unity is not a collapse of external quiddities but a unity in cognitive existence. This is philosophically subtle, but it is also vulnerable to misunderstanding.

The third tension concerns individuality after intellectual perfection. If perfected souls unite with the Active Intellect, how is individual identity preserved? Critics of Ṣadrā’s eschatology have pressed related questions about plurality in the immaterial realm and the status of souls after ascent. One Arabic critical discussion asks how plurality can remain in the imaginal or intellectual world if matter is the principle of numerical multiplicity.

The fourth tension concerns bodily resurrection. Ṣadrā wants to affirm bodily resurrection, but his resurrected body is not ordinary matter; it is an imaginal or soul-generated body. Some critics ask whether this satisfies the religious claim of bodily resurrection or replaces it with a philosophical reinterpretation. Turkish discussions similarly note that “non-material corporeality” can remain unclear.

The fifth tension concerns divine knowledge. Ṣadrā wants God’s knowledge to be simple, identical with God’s essence, and yet fully detailed with respect to all particulars. Persian discussions acknowledge that Ṣadrā’s texts can appear to give different formulations, especially regarding material particulars. This is not a defect that destroys the system, but it is a real exegetical and philosophical problem.

The sixth tension concerns public communicability. Knowledge by presence is immediate and non-representational, but philosophy is discursive and public. The Stanford discussion notes the problem: one may forge a language to discuss presential knowledge, but the experience itself is not informational in the ordinary public sense. Ṣadrā therefore needs a layered epistemology in which presential knowledge grounds certainty, while discursive knowledge communicates, interprets, and argues.

15. Final synthesis

Mullā Ṣadrā’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a metaphysics of conscious becoming. The human mind is not a static inner theater. It is the soul’s ascent from bodily life to self-presence, imagination, intellect, and possibly union with higher noetic realities. Perception is not passive reception but existential actualization. Imagination is not unreal fantasy but a real intermediate power. Self-knowledge is not inner observation but immediate presence. Learning is not information storage but transformation of being.

God and angels belong inside this philosophy of mind. God is the supreme case of simple, creative, non-representational knowledge: not a mind among minds, but the pure actuality and self-presence by which all finite cognition is ultimately intelligible. Angels and separate intellects are intermediate noetic realities: stable immaterial intellects that structure the intelligible order and serve as the horizon of human intellectual and prophetic perfection.

The result is a theory that is alien to many modern assumptions but philosophically coherent in its own terms. It rejects the reduction of mind to brain, but also rejects a crude soul–body dualism. It treats consciousness as graded presence, cognition as existential union, imagination as ontologically serious, and divine and angelic knowledge as essential to the full architecture of mind. A comprehensive Sadrian philosophy of mind would therefore be a theory of the soul’s intensification: from embodied potentiality, through sensory and imaginal cognition, into intellective presence, under the horizon of angelic intellect and divine knowledge.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
